breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: Inside serverQuery select new; serverQuery is materialized server-side at serverQuery.Count() (L1201) and serverQuery.ToList() (L1205) with NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable() boundary before the projection. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. The batch that split server/client lookups here left this DateTime helper in the SERVER projection. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs at line 1177, replace the untranslatable CLR-helper call. NOTE: DvDate is non-nullable DateTime, so do NOT use .Value on it (that is a compile error). Replace:

  validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.DvDate, (b.DvValidity == null) ? 0 : b.DvValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

with the EF-native form that translates to SQL DATEADD and preserves the exact comparison:

  validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.DvDate.AddDays((b.DvValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.DvValidity.Value + 1))) ? "Active" : "Expired",

(DvValidity is int?; .Value is safe inside the non-null branch. This keeps the original "Now <= DvDate + (validity+1) days" => Active/Expired logic identical to the EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays behavior.)
reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real EF Core 10 port-break.

SERVER-SIDE: serverQuery (Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs L1129) is a pure LINQ query expression over db.Deliverynotes with joins â€” one uninterrupted `from...select` block spanning L1129â€“1194 with NO .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable() boundary inside it. The line-1177 `validity` member is part of the `select new {...}` projection composed into the IQueryable. The FIRST materialization boundaries are serverQuery.Count() (L1201) and serverQuery.ToList() (L1205), both AFTER the projection. So L1177 executes on the SQL server. Not a standalone top-level query, not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Server-side: confirmed.

WOULD 500: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain static CLR helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (L70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0)`). I grepped the whole live tree: there is NO HasDbFunction registration for it in OnModelCreating â€” it is an unmapped CLR method. EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary static method call inside a server-side projection and throws "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. This is an unconditional projected column evaluated for EVERY row, so it fails on any non-empty result set (realistic data), not just edge data. Severity HIGH.

PORT REGRESSION vs legacy: EF6 supported the canonical DbFunctions.AddDays which mapped to SQL DATEADD; the port substituted the untranslatable DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays. The legacy DeliverynoteController is not present under _archive/legacy-mvc5 (couldn't diff that exact file), but the class is unambiguous and the equivalent translatable pattern (col).AddDays(n) has already been applied across sibling batches in this same repo. Genuine regression.

ONE CORRECTION TO THE HUNTER'S PROPOSED FIX: DvDate is declared NON-NULLABLE `public DateTime DvDate` (Models/Deliverynote.cs L13), so `b.DvDate.Value` would be a COMPILE ERROR (DateTime has no .Value). Use `b.DvDate` directly. DvValidity is `int?` (L41). EF Core 10 DOES translate DateTime.AddDays(double) to SQL DATEADD, so the EF-native form is the correct minimal output-preserving fix, preserving the original `DateTime.Now <= AddDays(...)` semantics.

breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: Inside select new of IQueryable v materialized by v.Count() (L2842) and v.ToList() (L2843); no AsEnumerable boundary before projection. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. (Same query also has hoe.ConvertFrom left-join unwrap and qs subquery issues, but the AddDays is the DateTime-helper break.) Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs at line 2817, replace the untranslatable CLR-shim call with EF-native DateTime.AddDays (which EF Core 10 translates to SQL DATEADD), preserving the legacy day computation exactly:

validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.DvDate.AddDays(b.DvValidity == null ? 0 : b.DvValidity.Value + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Notes:
- b.DvDate is non-nullable DateTime, so use b.DvDate.AddDays(...) (NOT .Value â€” DvDate has no .Value).
- The days argument resolves to a non-null int (0 when DvValidity is null, else DvValidity+1), exactly mirroring legacy DbFunctions.AddDays(b.DvDate, (b.DvValidity==null)?0:b.DvValidity+1).
- Keep the original comparison direction (DateTime.Now <= ...) so the Active/Expired output is byte-for-byte identical to the legacy EF6 result. No need to flip operands.

This same pattern recurs in the legacy file at L3104 (multiconvertdvnote/parallel grid); check the corresponding ported method in the live DeliverynoteController.cs for an identical DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays in another server-side select new and apply the same fix there.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED â€” independently verified all required conditions.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: In Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs, method MultipleDeliverynote(long? customer, long? type) (L2747, [HttpPost]/[QkAuthorize] action), the query `var v = (from b in db.Deliverynotes join ... select new { ... })` is built as a LINQ-to-Entities IQueryable rooted at EF DbSets (db.Deliverynotes/db.Customers/db.Employees/db.Users/db.HireDetails/db.ConvertTransactionss). The flagged projection at L2817 is inside that `select new`. There is NO .ToList()/.ToArray()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary between the query definition (L2782) and its enumeration at L2842 `recordsTotal = v.Count()` and L2843 `var data = v.ToList()` (the only .ToList() calls before then â€” L2771/2775/2777 â€” build the unrelated MCList/MCArray, not v). It is not a standalone top-level FirstOrDefault, not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. So the projection is translated to SQL by EF Core 10. Confirmed server-side.

(b) WOULD 500: `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain CLR static method in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (L68-71: `public static class DbFunctionsCompat { public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0); ... }`), explicitly commented "Compat for EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays (EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated)". It carries NO [DbFunction] attribute and is NOT on EF.Functions, so EF Core 10 has no translation for it. Calling it inside a server-side projection makes EF Core throw InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" â†’ HTTP 500. Because the expression is in the SELECT projection of EVERY returned row, it fails on any non-empty result set â€” high severity (500s on realistic data, not just edge data).

(c) PORT REGRESSION vs legacy: _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs L1435 and L3104 both use the canonical EF6 `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.DvDate, (b.DvValidity == null) ? 0 : b.DvValidity + 1)`, which EF6 translates to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped in the untranslatable CLR shim DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays. Genuine regression.

Entity types: Deliverynote.DvDate is non-nullable `DateTime` (Models/Deliverynote.cs L13); DvValidity is `int?` (L41). So the EF-native translatable form is `b.DvDate.AddDays(n)` where n is a non-null int â€” DateTime.AddDays IS translated by EF Core's SQL Server provider to DATEADD.

Note on the hunter's proposed fix: it is on the right track but has two flaws â€” (1) it writes `b.DvDate.Value.AddDays(...)`, but DvDate is non-nullable DateTime and has no `.Value` (compile error); (2) it writes `(b.DvValidity + 1).Value` as the days arg, which still leaves a nullable; cleaner to coalesce to a non-null int. My finalFix corrects both while preserving the exact legacy semantics (Active when now <= DvDate + (validity==null?0:validity+1) days).

breakClass: left-join null member access / non-nullable value-column read on a possibly-null DefaultIfEmpty() entity in a server-side projection (EF Core 10 shaper reads SQL NULL into a non-nullable CLR value type â€” decimal/int/DateTime/enum â€” and throws; EF6 silently produced default(T))
why: c=Customers left join; Customer.CreditPeriod is non-nullable int. Walking/orphaned customer -> c NULL -> 500. Server-side. Legacy identical. Representative of the CreditPeriod=party.CreditPeriod class (CreditSale/ProForma/PurchaseOrder/Common.cs â€” see notes). MED.
finalFix:
In QuickNetCore/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs, line 1358, change:

    CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod,

to:

    CreditPeriod = c != null ? c.CreditPeriod : 0,

This mirrors the legacy EF6 behavior (which silently yielded default(int)=0 for the unmatched/NULL left-joined customer). The `c != null` guard is translated by EF Core into a CASE/null-check so the non-nullable int is never read from a SQL NULL, preserving the original output (0) for walking/orphaned-customer delivery notes while keeping the real CreditPeriod for matched customers. The same one-line guard should be applied to the sibling CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod sites in Models/Common.cs (lines 8625, 11308, 12365, 12589, 12807) which share the identical left-join-then-read pattern.
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the flag rather than refuted it. Location: QuickNetCore/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs, method Details(long? id), line 1358: `CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod`.

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The projection is part of one continuous LINQ query (`from b in db.Deliverynotes ... select new DeliverynoteViewModel {...}`) whose only materialization boundary is `.FirstOrDefault()` at line 1382. There is no prior `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` and it is not a standalone top-level local query nor a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. The whole select is translated to SQL, so `c.CreditPeriod` is read by the EF Core shaper from the SQL result.

(b) WOULD 500 ON REALISTIC/EDGE DATA â€” CONFIRMED. `c` originates from `join c in db.Customers on b.Customer equals c.CustomerID into cust / from c in cust.DefaultIfEmpty()` (lines 1328-1329), i.e. an optional (left-joined) entity that is NULL when no customer matches. `Customer.CreditPeriod` is a NON-nullable `int` (Models/Customer.cs line 26). `Deliverynote.Customer` is a non-nullable `long` FK, and the same projection's PayType branch (`b.CustomerType == CustomerType.Walking ? "Cash" : "Credit"`) plus the CustomerType enum (General.cs: Walking/Card = cash) prove walking/cash-sale delivery notes â€” which carry no real Customer row â€” are an expected, real scenario. For such a row the left join yields no match, `c` is NULL, and the SQL column for `c.CreditPeriod` comes back NULL. EF Core 10's shaper reads that SQL NULL into the non-nullable CLR `int` and throws (InvalidOperationException / null-at-ordinal cast failure) -> HTTP 500. EF6's legacy ObjectQuery shaper silently produced default(int)=0, so the page rendered.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. The legacy EF6 source at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/DeliverynoteController.cs (line 1580, inside the identical Details query with the same `from c in cust.DefaultIfEmpty()` left join) is byte-identical: `CreditPeriod = c.CreditPeriod`. The code was not changed during the port; only the EF runtime behavior changed. This is a faithful member of the CreditPeriod=party.CreditPeriod class noted in project memory (batch 27 / CreditSale/ProForma/PurchaseOrder/Common.cs counterparts at Common.cs lines 8625, 11308, 12365, 12589, 12807, etc.).

Severity: MED â€” it 500s only when a walking/cash-sale or orphaned-customer delivery note is opened in Details (empty left-join row), not on every credit-customer note. The agent's analysis is accurate; nothing to refute.
